source_notes: A18R selector-rail transfer fanout derived from the selector-rail bounded survivor.
This pool tests whether peripeteia-style rhetorical memory transfers across nearby constructed-device siblings.

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The top token has a gold fleck matching the final badge. The middle token is closest to the badge. The bottom rail has the cleanest groove but no matching token. A small green tab sits beside the middle rail.
cards.learner: I want the top token for the gold match, but the middle one is closer. The groove clue does not even sit on the token, so I am not sure what to trust.
cards.stasis_question: which visible cue is allowed to govern the others
cards.brief_answer: test the frame mark as a cue about which rail has authority
cards.near_analog: using a legend key before judging the colors on a map
cards.micro_task: point to the small frame mark, then point to the rail it singles out
cards.wrong_turn: adding color, nearness, and groove as equal votes
cards.hidden_relation: one cue may be a selector for the cue space rather than another competing cue
cards.voiced_position: I can compare the clues, but comparison only makes three pulls
cards.correction: ask which clue controls the frame before weighing the others

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The bottom token has a blue fleck matching the final badge. The middle token is nearest to the badge. The top rail has the clearest groove but a small blue tab sits beside the top rail.
cards.learner: Blue match says bottom, nearness says middle, and the clean groove is on top. The little tab is also up there, but I do not know whether it is just decoration.
cards.stasis_question: which mark is selecting the rail rather than competing as another token clue
cards.brief_answer: make the tab govern the rail before comparing token features
cards.near_analog: reading the row label before ranking the entries in a table
cards.micro_task: point to the blue tab, then point to the rail it names
cards.wrong_turn: treating the tab, color, closeness, and groove as four equal votes
cards.hidden_relation: the tab is a rail selector, not another token-quality clue
cards.voiced_position: Every clue pulls somewhere, so I keep counting votes
cards.correction: test the equal-vote idea first, then let the tab name the rail

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The upper token has a plum fleck matching the final badge. The lower token is closest to the badge. The middle rail has the smoothest groove. A small white tab sits beside the lower rail.
cards.learner: The plum color makes me want the upper token, the closest one is lower, and the smooth groove is middle. The white tab by the lower rail might matter, but I cannot tell why.
cards.stasis_question: which clue changes the kind of choice being made
cards.brief_answer: use the frame tab to choose the rail before weighing token surface cues
cards.near_analog: checking which shelf label applies before choosing the nicest book cover
cards.micro_task: touch the white tab, then name the rail it marks
cards.wrong_turn: ranking attractive token features before finding the rail selector
cards.hidden_relation: the tab changes the question from best-looking token to selected rail
cards.voiced_position: I can rank the clues, but the ranking keeps changing
cards.correction: switch from ranking features to locating the selector mark

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The lower token has a rust fleck matching the final badge. The top token sits closest to the badge. The lower rail is the cleanest path. A small rust tab is mounted beside the middle rail.
cards.learner: Rust and the clean path both pull me down, but the closest token is up. The rust tab is in the middle, so I do not know whether color is the clue or the tab is.
cards.stasis_question: what happens if matching color is treated as the whole rule
cards.brief_answer: make color-match fail as a complete rule before using the tab as selector
cards.near_analog: testing a shortcut on the counterexample before trusting it
cards.micro_task: say where the color rule points, then where the tab points
cards.wrong_turn: letting matching color override the rail-marking tab
cards.hidden_relation: the same color can mislead when one instance marks a token and the other marks a rail
cards.voiced_position: If rust matters, I cannot tell which rust thing matters
cards.correction: separate token color from rail marker before choosing

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The middle token has a teal fleck matching the final badge. The lower token is closest to the badge. The middle groove is cleanest, but a small square notch sits beside the top rail.
cards.learner: Two clues point middle, one points lower, and the square notch points top if notches count. It feels like middle wins unless the notch changes the rule.
cards.stasis_question: when does a minority cue govern the whole cue space
cards.brief_answer: test whether the notch is a rail selector rather than another vote
cards.near_analog: letting the scale key set the units before counting marks
cards.micro_task: point to the square notch, then point to the rail it selects
cards.wrong_turn: using majority vote when one cue sets the voting rule
cards.hidden_relation: the selector can override a majority of surface cues because it names the rail under inspection
cards.voiced_position: Middle has more evidence unless the notch is a different kind of evidence
cards.correction: ask whether the notch sets the rail before counting the other clues

cards.stage: A slate card has three rails. The top token is closest to the badge. The middle token has a gold fleck matching the badge. The top rail has the cleanest groove. A small black tab sits beside the lower rail.
cards.learner: Closest and cleanest both say top, gold says middle, and the black tab says lower if it counts. I do not see why one little tab can beat two clues.
cards.stasis_question: what kind of cue can beat a surface majority
cards.brief_answer: show that the majority cues do not answer which rail the card marks
cards.near_analog: following a street sign even when two storefronts look more familiar
cards.micro_task: name the two top-pulling cues, then name the rail the tab marks
cards.wrong_turn: letting a surface majority answer a selector question
cards.hidden_relation: the black tab is not a weak vote; it changes which rail is in question
cards.voiced_position: I can count clues, but I cannot tell whether counting is allowed
cards.correction: make counting fail as the rule before using the selector tab
